methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 22.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $5B

private.name: d-Matrix
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $2B

private.name: Substrate
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $1B+

private.name: Rain AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
Mindgrove Technologies is a Series A chip design company in the Semiconductors sector that has raised ₹85 Cr (~$10M) to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of 4 private peers at identical stage and sector: Etched ($5.0B), d-Matrix ($2.0B), Substrate ($1.0B), and Rain AI ($500M). A public comp set of 3 major semiconductor players (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) exists but cannot be applied due to absence of target revenue disclosure.

Peer-median valuation represents the primary applicable method. The 4-company peer cohort yields a median valuation of $1.5B, with a range spanning $500M to $5.0B. This method directly reflects transaction and funding rounds for companies operating in identical verticals and development stage, making it the most grounded triangulation point available given data constraints.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $1.3B to $1.7B, centered on a $1.5B median. Key uncertainties include the significant dispersion within the peer set ($5.0B gap between high and low comps), absence of revenue-based anchoring via public markets multiples, and limited visibility into Mindgrove's proprietary technology differentiation relative to peers. A public-comp approach remains inaccessible pending revenue disclosure. The single-method conclusion underscores reliance on peer comparables alone.
